Although the Syrians were idolaters and oppressed God’s people, the deliverance in which Naaman had been involved is here attributed to the Lord. This is the correct language of Scripture; those who write common history plainly show that God is not in all their thoughts. No one's greatness or honor can place him beyond the reach of severe hardships in life; there are many with sickly, frail bodies under rich and fine clothing. Every person has some flaw or other, something that blemishes and diminishes them, some imperfection to their grandeur, some discouragement to their joy. This little girl, though only a child, could give an account of the famous prophet the Israelites had among them. Children should be told early about the wondrous works of God so that, wherever they go, they may talk about them. As became a good servant, she desired the health and welfare of her master, even though she was a captive, a servant by force; much more should servants by choice seek their masters' good. Servants can be blessings to families where they are, by sharing what they know about the glory of God and the honor of his prophets. Naaman did not despise what she told him because of her low status. It would be well if people were as aware of the burden of sin as they are of bodily illness. And when they seek the blessings that the Lord sends in response to the prayers of his faithful people, they will find nothing can be obtained unless they come as beggars for a free gift, not as lords demanding or purchasing it.

Elisha knew Naaman to be a proud man, and he would let him know that before the great God all people stand on the same level. All of God’s commands test people's spirits, especially those that direct a sinner how to seek the blessings of salvation. See in Naaman the folly of pride; a cure will not satisfy him unless he is cured with pomp and display. He refuses to be healed unless it is done in a way that pleases him. The way by which a sinner is received and made holy, through the blood and by the Spirit of Christ, through faith alone in his name, does not sufficiently satisfy or employ self to please the sinner's heart. Human wisdom thinks it can supply wiser and better methods of cleansing. Observe that masters should be willing to hear reason. As we should be deaf to the counsel of the ungodly, even when given by great and respected people, so we are to have our ears open to good advice, even if brought by those below us. Would you do anything at all? When diseased sinners are content to do anything, to submit to anything, to give up anything, for a cure, then, and not until then, is there any hope for them. The methods for healing the leprosy of sin are so clear that we have no excuse if we do not follow them. It is simply: Believe, and be saved; Repent, and be pardoned; Wash, and be clean. The believer asks for salvation, without neglecting, altering, or adding to the Savior's instructions; he is thus made clean from guilt, while others, who neglect them, live and die in the leprosy of sin.

The mercy of the cure affected Naaman more than the miracle itself. Those are best able to speak of the power of divine grace who themselves experience it. He also shows himself grateful to Elisha the prophet. Elijah refused any reward, not because he thought it unlawful—for he received gifts from others—but to show this new convert that servants of the God of Israel looked upon worldly wealth with a holy contempt. The entire work was from God in such a way that the prophet would not give counsel unless he had directions from the Lord. It is not wise to violently oppose minor errors which accompany people’s first convictions; we cannot advance people any faster than the Lord prepares them to receive instruction. However, if, when making a covenant with God, we desire to reserve some known sin or continue indulging ourselves in it, that is a breach of his covenant. Those who truly hate evil will be careful to abstain from all appearances of evil.

Naaman, a Syrian, a courtier, a soldier, had many servants, and we read how wise and good they were. Elisha, a holy prophet, a man of God, has only one servant, and he proves to be a deceitful liar. The love of money, that root of all evil, was at the bottom of Gehazi's sin. He thought to deceive the prophet but soon found that the Spirit of prophecy could not be deceived, and it was futile to lie to the Holy Ghost. It is foolish to presume upon sin in hopes of secrecy. When you go aside into any byway, does not your own conscience go with you? Does not the eye of God go with you? Whoever covers his sin will not prosper; particularly, a lying tongue lasts only for a moment. All the foolish hopes and schemes of worldly people are open before God. It is not a time to increase our wealth when we can only do so in ways that dishonor God and religion or harm others. Gehazi was punished. If he wants Naaman’s money, he will have his disease with it. What did Gehazi gain even though he obtained two talents, when thereby he lost his health, his honor, his peace, his service, and, if repentance prevented not, his soul forever? Let us beware of hypocrisy and covetousness, and dread the curse of spiritual leprosy remaining on our souls.
